title: The Epic of Geser Khan
summary: Geser Khan is the central hero of one of the great Mongolian epics. Born as a runny-nosed, despised child on the Mongolian steppe, he is actually the son of the sky-deity Khürmasta Tengri, sent to earth to defeat the demons (mangus) that are devouring the world. Despite being mocked and rejected, Geser grows to defeat multi-headed ogres, rescue his mother from the underworld, marry the celestial princess Rogmo Goa, and subdue the entire universe including heaven and earth. The epic survives in Mongolian, Tibetan, Buryat, and Altai versions, with the earliest Mongolian printed edition from 1716 in Beijing.

title: The Origin of the Horsehead Fiddle (Morin Khuur)
summary: A shepherd named Namjil the Cuckoo from eastern Mongolia was drafted into military service on the western border. He sang so beautifully that people called him Namjil the Cuckoo. During his service, he fell in love with a local prince's daughter, who gave him a magical winged horse to cross the vast distances to meet her. A jealous woman discovered the horse's wings and cut them off, causing the horse to fall from the sky and die. The grieving Namjil made the first horsehead fiddle from the horse's skin, tail hair, and bones, using it to play poignant songs about his beloved horse and lost love. The instrument became the national symbol of Mongolia.

title: The Golden Frog Creation Myth
summary: In the beginning, the earth was submerged in a boundless ocean. When the lord of the universe, Buddha Sakyamuni, flew over the ocean seeking to create land, he saw a golden-bodied frog swimming from north to south. Using his bow and arrows, he shot the frog's east side, turning it northward. Fire gushed from its mouth and water from its rump. Buddha threw golden sand on the frog, which became the earth. The arrow's protruding part became a forest, the arrowhead that passed through became a metal area. The fire from the frog's mouth made the north a region of fire, and the water from its rump made the south a watery area. When the frog moves its legs or shakes its head, earthquakes result.

title: Erkei-Mergen and the Seven Suns
summary: Long ago, seven suns rose in the sky simultaneously, drying up all rivers and vegetation on earth. Men and animals struggled to survive in the unbearable heat. A famous archer named Erkei-Mergen stepped forward to save the world. Using his divine bow, he shot down the extra suns one by one, restoring balance to the world. The story explains the origin of the single sun and moon in the sky and is one of the most widespread Mongol folktales about the regulation of celestial bodies, reflecting the ancient Mongols' interest in natural phenomena and their origins.

title: The Blue Wolf and the Fallow Doe: Ancestors of the Mongols
summary: According to the Secret History of the Mongols, the Mongol people descend from a blue wolf (Börte Chino) and a fallow doe (Göo Maral) sent by Tengri (Heaven). The wolf and doe crossed the Tengis Sea and settled near the source of the Onon River at Mount Burkhan Khaldun, where they gave birth to the first Mongol, named Batachiqan. This legendary origin story establishes the divine mandate of the Mongol ruling line and the sacred landscape of the Onon and Kherlen river region. The blue wolf ancestress myth was central to Genghis Khan's claim of heavenly authority to unite the Mongol tribes.

title: The Legend of the Singing Sands of the Gobi
summary: In the heart of the Gobi Desert lies the Khongoryn Els — the Singing Sands. According to Mongol legend, these massive sand dunes were once a great city ruled by a cruel khan. The khan oppressed his people so terribly that the sky god Tengri cursed the city, burying it under mountains of sand. The souls of the buried inhabitants still cry out beneath the dunes, producing the deep, resonant hum that travelers hear when the wind blows across the sand. Mongol herders say the sands sing loudest before storms, warning travelers of danger ahead.

title: Mandukhai the Wise Queen
summary: Mandukhai Khatun (also known as Mandukhai Sechen Khongorjin) was a 15th-century Mongol queen who reunified the fractured Mongol nation after the fall of the Yuan dynasty. According to legend, she raised the orphaned Dayan Khan from childhood, then married him when he came of age. At age thirty-two, she led the Mongol army into battle against the Oirats while pregnant, wearing armor and carrying twin swords. She defeated the Oirat forces and secured Dayan Khan's rule. Her wisdom in statecraft and courage in battle made her one of the most revered figures in Mongol history, embodying the ideal of the warrior-queen.

title: The Deer Stone Legends
summary: Across the Mongolian steppe stand ancient megalithic Deer Stones — carved monoliths dating back 3,000 years to the Bronze Age. Mongol legend says these stones are warriors turned to stone by the sky god Tengri for their arrogance. The deer depicted leaping across the stones represent the souls of fallen warriors ascending to the heavens. Another legend holds that the stones mark the graves of great shamans, with the deer serving as spirit animals to carry the shaman's soul across the sky. The most famous concentration is at the Uushigiin Uvur site in Khövsgöl Province, where over a dozen Deer Stones form a sacred ceremonial complex.

title: Erlig Khan and the Palace of the Dead
summary: Erlig Khan (also Erlik Khan) is the Mongol and Turkic god of death and ruler of the underworld. In one of the most famous Mongol tales, 'The Woman Who Sought Her Husband in the Palace of Erlik Khan,' a brave woman descends into the underworld to rescue her dead husband. She navigates past guardian spirits and offers tribute at the court of the terrifying Erlig Khan, who sits in judgment over the souls of the dead. Through her courage and devotion, she wins her husband's soul back. The story reflects the Mongol belief in a multi-layered cosmos where the underworld is a place of judgment but accessible through shamanic journey and pure intent.

title: Genghis Khan and the Golden Whip
summary: According to Mongol legend, the young Temüjin (later Genghis Khan) was riding alone in the steppe when he discovered a golden whip lying on the ground. When he picked it up, the sky thundered and Tengri spoke, telling him that this whip was a sign that he would unite all the Mongol tribes under one banner. The whip had the power to command the loyalty of all who saw it. Genghis Khan kept the golden whip as his personal standard (the nine-tailed white banner) and carried it into every battle. The whip became a sacred symbol of Mongol unity and imperial authority, kept to this day as a national relic of Mongolia.
source: Mongolian oral tradition, Secret History of the Mongols

title: The Epic of Jangar
summary: Jangar is a heroic epic of the Oirat Mongols (Kalmyks), comprising hundreds of thousands of lines. The epic describes the ideal kingdom of Bumba, ruled by the heroic Jangar and his twelve brave warriors (including the invincible Hongor, the swift Sabar, and the strong Mingyan). The heroes protect Bumba from monstrous invaders, capture magical horses and beautiful maidens, and embody the highest ideals of Mongol warrior culture — loyalty, courage, and hospitality. The epic is traditionally sung with tovshuur (lute) accompaniment during celebrations. The kingdom of Bumba represents a paradise on earth where there is no death, suffering, or old age.

title: Korkut Ata and the Search for Immortality
summary: Korkut Ata (also Dede Korkut) is the legendary father of Kazakh music and the creator of the kobyz (horsehair string instrument). Unable to accept the inevitability of death, Korkut fled from human society into nature, seeking immortality. But every element of nature — the trees, mountains, steppes, and rivers — told him that they too were subject to decay and death. In his solitary anguish, Korkut hollowed out a tree called shirgay to make the first kobyz, pulled horsehair strings across it, and began to play. The music was so transcendent that it brought him immortality through art. His melodies still echo across the Kazakh steppe, and his burial place on the Syr Darya riverbank is a pilgrimage site.

title: Alpamysh Batyr
summary: Alpamysh (Alpamıs in Kazakh) is one of the most important Turkic epic heroes, shared across Kazakh, Uzbek, Karakalpak, and Altai traditions. The epic tells of the hero's miraculous birth to elderly parents after years of prayer, his childhood exploits, and his marriage to the beautiful Barchin after defeating rival suitors in contests. When Barchin's father moves the clan to the land of the Kalmyks, Alpamysh follows and wins her through heroic feats. After returning home, he fights the usurper Ultantaz who has persecuted his people and tried to force Barchin into marriage. The epic ends with the reunification of the divided Kongrat tribe under Alpamysh's heroic leadership.

title: Qozy Körpesh and Bayan Sulu
summary: The oldest Kazakh love dastan, dating back over 1,500 years to the era of the Türgesh Khaganate. Qozy and Bayan are promised to each other from birth, but Qozy's father dies before the marriage can take place. Bayan's ambitious father, Karabai, breaks the promise and betroths her to a wealthy rival. Qozy searches for his beloved across the vast steppe, overcoming obstacles imposed by Bayan's family. The lovers are reunited, but a tragic fate awaits them — Qozy is murdered by Kokman, a servant of Bayan's unwanted suitor. Bayan, in her grief, avenges Qozy's death and then takes her own life to join her beloved in death. Their shared grave is marked by a twelve-meter stone mausoleum standing in the Ayagoz district of Kazakhstan.

title: The Legend of Asan Kaighy and the Promised Land
summary: Asan Kaighy ('Asan the Sorrowful') is a legendary figure said to have lived during the reign of Kazakh Khan Janibek in the 15th century. A wise counselor and prophet, Asan searched across the entire Kazakh steppe for a promised land where there would be no suffering, hatred, or injustice — a paradise on earth where skylarks would nest peacefully among the sheep. He traveled on his swift camel, Jhelmaia, covering all the steppes, but could never find this utopia. Before dying, he promised to lead his people to unknown lands with better conditions, but death took him before he could fulfill his promise. His story embodies the eternal Kazakh yearning for justice and a better life.

title: The Story of Ablai Khan
summary: Ablai Khan (1711-1781) was one of the greatest Kazakh khans, who united the three Kazakh zhuzes (hordes) and led the struggle against the Dzungar invaders. According to legend, as a thirteen-year-old orphan named Abulmansur, he worked as a shepherd disguised as a beggar called 'Sabalak' to avoid his father's rivals. His destiny was revealed when he miraculously defeated a Dzungar champion in single combat, earning the title 'batyr' (hero). He was a master diplomat who played Russia against China to maintain Kazakh independence. Legend says he was blessed by the spirit of his ancestor Genghis Khan and commanded the loyalty of all the steppe tribes through a combination of military genius and sacred charisma.

title: The Legend of Alasha Khan and the Three Zhuzes
summary: Long ago, a khan's son was born with a brindled (striped) appearance. Ashamed, the khan ordered the child abandoned in the steppe. An old woman collecting dung found the child and raised him. The boy grew into a young man of exceptional fairness, dexterity, strength, and wisdom. When the khan heard of his remarkable son, he sent a hundred warriors to bring him back, but they were so impressed by the young man that they stayed with him instead. This happened three times, with three hundred warriors choosing to serve him. They lifted him on white felt and proclaimed him Alasha Khan. He organized them into three groups, which became the three zhuzes (Senior, Middle, Junior) of the Kazakh people. This is why the battle cry of all Kazakhs is 'Alash!'

title: Aldar Qose the Trickster
summary: Aldar Qose is the beloved Kazakh trickster figure, analogous to Nasreddin Hodja in other Turkic traditions. He is a poor but clever wanderer who uses his wit to outsmart greedy bais (wealthy men), corrupt officials, and even devils. In one famous tale, Aldar convinces a miserly bai to give him almost all his possessions — including his beautiful daughter — through elaborate verbal tricks. In another, a devil who comes to take possession of Aldar ends up outsmarted and humiliated. Aldar Qose stories serve as social satire, exposing the hypocrisy of the powerful while celebrating the cunning of the common people.

title: The Forty Robbers of the Steppe (Kirk Kazak)
summary: The legend of the 'Forty Kazakhs' (Kirk Kazak) tells of forty exiled warriors who formed a brotherhood on the Kazakh steppe. According to tradition, the sons of a khan's younger wife, offended by their older brothers, fled to the steppe with thirty-three other exiles. They banded together as forty warriors, defending travelers on the Silk Road and fighting for justice. Their leader was a just and courageous batyr, and their code of honor required absolute loyalty to each other and protection of the weak. This legend is considered one of the foundational stories of Kazakh identity — the number forty appears throughout Kazakh symbolism, and the warriors' code influenced the Kazakh concept of heroism and justice.

title: The Epic of Manas: The Birth of the Hero
summary: The Epic of Manas is the longest epic poem in the world (500,000+ lines), central to Kyrgyz national identity. Manas was born to the elderly and childless Jakyp in exile on the Altai. His birth was marked by supernatural signs — a mysterious light filled the tent, the earth trembled, and the sky thundered. Even as an infant, Manas showed extraordinary strength, weighing as much as a thirty-year-old man and greedily eating three sheep stomachs full of butter. From childhood, he gathered forty companions (kyrk choro) and began rallying the scattered Kyrgyz tribes against their Kalmyk and Chinese oppressors. The epic's reciters (manaschi) are believed to receive their calling through dreams, where Manas's spirit visits them.

title: Manas: The Great Campaign and Death of the Hero
summary: The second major cycle of the Manas epic describes how Manas united the forty Kyrgyz tribes under the Tengrist sky-banner and led them to reclaim the Ala-Too mountains from Chinese and Kalmyk invaders. His magical white stallion Ak-Kula carried him through epic battles, advising him with human speech and prophetic warnings. After the great campaign against Chinese forces led by Konurbai, Manas was mortally wounded by treachery. His wife Kanykey buried him and built a mausoleum (Gumbez Manasa) over his grave in the Talas valley. To protect the tomb from grave-robbers, she inscribed a decoy woman's name on it. The mausoleum, dating from 1334, remains the most sacred pilgrimage site in Kyrgyzstan.

title: Semetey: The Son of Manas
summary: After Manas's death, his treacherous younger brothers Abyke and Bobosh seized power and tried to kill Manas's son Semetey. Kanykey, Manas's widow, escaped with the infant Semetey to Bukhara, where her father ruled as khan. Semetey grew up unaware of his heritage until his mother revealed his identity. He returned to the Talas valley on the magical horse Taytoru to avenge his father's death. Semetey defeated his treacherous uncles in battle and beheaded the forty companions of his father who had betrayed Manas. He married the beautiful Ay-Chürök, a swan maiden, and continued his father's legacy of defending the Kyrgyz people. The second part of the Manas trilogy bears his name.

title: The Legend of Lake Issyk-Kul
summary: Lake Issyk-Kul, the second-largest mountain lake in the world, is the subject of numerous Kyrgyz legends. One legend tells of a cruel khan who fell in love with a beautiful shepherdess. When she rejected him, he kidnapped her. Her lover, riding a magical Tulpar (winged horse), rescued her but she was recaptured. Rather than submit to the khan, she threw herself from a tower. As punishment for her death, clear mountain water rushed down and flooded the entire valley where the khan's palace stood, creating the vast lake. Another legend says that Timur (Tamerlane) required each of his soldiers to place a stone at Issyk-Kul's shore to count his army, creating the Santash stone ridge. Some Kyrgyz legends even claim Genghis Khan is buried beneath the lake's waters.

title: The Creation of Lake Issyk-Kul
summary: An alternate legend describes the origin of Issyk-Kul ('Warm Lake') as the tears of the heavens. An old man and his beautiful daughter lived on the shores of a small river. When invaders came, the daughter prayed to Tengri for salvation. The sky god answered by sending a flood that drowned the invaders but also the old man. The daughter's endless tears of grief filled the valley, forming the vast lake that never freezes despite its altitude. The warm waters represent the mother's eternal love and the father's enduring protection. Even today, Kyrgyz herders say the lake's surface reflects the faces of those who died in the flood.

title: The Epic of Alpamysh (Uzbek Version)
summary: The Uzbek version of Alpamysh (Alpomish) is the national heroic epic of Uzbekistan, with the largest recorded version by bard Fazil Yuldashogli containing 14,000 verses. The epic follows Alpamysh from his miraculous birth to elderly parents through his heroic courtship of Barchin, whom he wins in suitor contests. After marrying Barchin and returning home, he fights the usurper Ultantaz who has persecuted his father and tried to force Barchin into marriage. Alpamysh, returning after seven years in disguise as his old shepherd Kultay, comes to the wedding feast, frees his wife, and kills Ultantaz. The epic ends with reunification of the divided Kongrat tribe. The epic is performed by bakhshi (bardic artists) to the accompaniment of the dombra.

title: The Legend of Bibi-Khanum
summary: Bibi-Khanum, the favorite wife of Emir Timur (Tamerlane), decided to surprise her husband with a magnificent mosque while he was away at war. Thousands of architects and builders created a masterpiece of sapphire and turquoise. But the main architect fell madly in love with Bibi-Khanum and refused to complete the work unless she allowed him to kiss her. To finish the gift before Timur returned, she consented — but through her veil. The kiss was so passionate it left a trace on her cheek. Timur noticed the mark upon his return but said nothing until he brought her to the top of the mosque and pushed her to her death. He grieved deeply but named the mosque after her. Another version says the architect grew wings and flew to Mashhad to escape Timur's justice.

title: The Legend of the Singing Well of Khiva
summary: In the ancient city of Khiva, there is a well that is said to 'sing' when the wind blows across its opening. According to legend, a beautiful slave girl named Zulfiya was imprisoned in the well by a cruel khan for refusing his advances. She sang day and night, her voice echoing through the depths. Even after her death, her spirit continues to sing. Local tradition holds that the well's song changes according to the listener's fate — joyful melodies for those destined for happiness, mournful songs for those facing hardship. The legend is part of the rich oral tradition of Khorezm, the historical region that was once a center of Zoroastrian culture and later Islamic scholarship.

title: Aina-Kizz and the Black-Bearded Bai
summary: Aina-Kizz, an incredibly clever young girl from Samarkand, helps her widowed mother by using her intelligence to outwit a greedy black-bearded bai (rich man). The bai boasts of impossible feats — that he once grew a wheat field so vast that a whole caravan got lost in it, and that the caravan emerged from his mouth when he ate bread. Aina-Kizz cleverly exposes his lies and reveals that he is the murderer of her uncle, whose silk robe the bai still wears. Through her wit and careful trap-setting, she forces the bai to confess and returns justice to her family. The story celebrates feminine intelligence over male arrogance and remains one of the most popular Uzbek folk tales.

title: The Epic of Amir Timur's Rise
summary: According to Uzbek legend, the great conqueror Amir Timur (Tamerlane) was born with a clot of dried blood in his fist — a sign of his destiny as a warrior. As a young man, he was a sheep thief and a bandit until a vision from the spirit of his ancestor showed him his true path. After being wounded in battle, he limped through the mountains and took refuge in the cave of a holy man who prophesied that Timur would conquer from Damascus to Delhi. Timur's rise from obscure origins to ruler of the mightiest empire since Genghis Khan is seen as proof of divine favor. His construction projects in Samarkand, especially the Registan and Bibi-Khanum mosque, were said to be built with the help of jinn spirits.

title: Göroglu: The Son of the Grave (Turkmen Version)
summary: The Turkmen epic of Göroglu ('Son of the Grave') — also known as Köroğlu — is the national epic of Turkmenistan, recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage. Unlike the western versions, the Turkmen Göroglu was born miraculously in his mother's grave after she died, giving him his name. He was raised by his grandfather Jygalybek and aunt Gülendam, grew up with superhuman strength, and captured the magical winged horse Gyrat. With his forty jigits (warriors), Göroglu built the fortress of Chandybil and defended the Turkmen people against foreign oppressors. He married the peri (fairy) Agaýunus after seeing her in a dream. The epic combines heroic battles, romance, and deep mythological motifs including the wise figure Hızır who guides the hero.

title: The Legend of the Akhal-Teke Horse
summary: The Akhal-Teke horse, Turkmenistan's national treasure, is the subject of a creation legend. According to Turkmen tradition, the first Akhal-Teke was born from the union of a snake, a cheetah, and an eagle — explaining the horse's serpentine neck, cheetah-like speed, and eagle-eyed alertness. The breed's distinctive metallic golden coat came from the sun god's blessing. The horses were considered 'Heavenly Horses' by the Chinese, who believed they sweated blood (actually a trick of light on their thin skin). Legend says the Akhal-Teke can travel 300 miles through the Karakum Desert without water, guided by the spirits of their ancestors. The bond between horse and rider is so sacred that the horse is considered a member of the family, tied beside the yurt and fed bread soaked in mutton fat.

title: The Creation of the Karakum Desert
summary: According to Turkmen legend, the Karakum ('Black Sands') Desert was created by a battle between good and evil forces. The devil challenged the creator god to a contest — who could cover more land in one night. The creator god began covering the land with fertile soil, but the devil raised giant sandstorms. The creator god, realizing the devil's trick, scattered black stones across the sand to stop his advance. These stones became the desert's namesake 'black sands.' Another legend says that a giant serpent slept beneath the land, and when it turned in its sleep, it churned the earth into sand. The few oases that survive in the desert were created where the tears of angels fell, grieving for the land's desolation. The Bakhshi singers still perform tales of how the Turkmen people learned to thrive in this harsh environment.

title: Zuleikha and the Forty Maidens
summary: The legend of the Forty Maidens (Kirk Kiz) is a Central Asian heroic tale found among Turkmen, Kazakh, and Karakalpak peoples. A beautiful and brave maiden named Zuleikha leads a band of forty warrior women who defend their people against invading forces. Rather than submit to forced marriage to an enemy khan, Zuleikha trains herself and her forty companions in martial arts, horseback riding, and archery. The forty women fight with the ferocity of lions and the cunning of wolves. When the enemy surrounds their fortress, Zuleikha leads a night raid that scatters the invaders. The stone monument 'Forty Girls' exists in multiple locations across Central Asia, believed to be the petrified remains of these warrior women who chose death over dishonor. The legend celebrates female heroism and resistance against oppression.

title: Shurale the Forest Spirit
summary: Shurale is the most famous mythological creature in Tatar folklore — a forest spirit depicted as a hairy man with a horn on his forehead, one eye, and extremely long fingers. Shurale lures travelers into the forest, rides their horses at night, and tickles his victims to death. The most famous story, immortalized by Tatar poet Gabdulla Tukay, tells of a young woodcutter who outwits Shurale by tricking him into inserting his fingers into a split log, then removing the wedge to trap him. When Shurale screams in pain, other forest spirits ask what happened, and he tells them 'Byltyr kysty' (Last year pinched me) — so afraid of admitting he was fooled. The story teaches cleverness over brute strength and remains a cornerstone of Tatar cultural identity.

title: Su Anasi: The Water Mother
summary: Su Anasi ('Mother of Water') is the Tatar water spirit, analogous to the Russian rusalka but with distinct Tatar characteristics. Long-haired and beautiful, she lives in rivers and lakes, with a golden comb that she uses to brush her hair while sitting on riverbanks. The most famous version, retold by poet Gabdulla Tukay, describes a village boy who finds Su Anasi's golden comb by the river and runs away with it. The water mother chases him all the way to the village until the village dogs drive her away. That night, she comes to his window, tapping and demanding her comb back. The boy's mother throws the comb out the window to appease her. The story teaches children not to take things that don't belong to them, as every object has a spirit owner ('iyäsi') who will reclaim it.

title: Chura-Batyr: The Hero of Kazan
summary: Chura-Batyr is the heroic epic of the Tatar people, set in the mid-16th century during the Kazan Khanate's struggle against the expanding Moscow state. Chura is a batyr (hero) who defends Kazan against Russian invasion. The epic contains ancient motifs including Chura's miraculous birth to elderly parents after years of childlessness, his heroic childhood, his journey to Kazan with the blessing of a holy elder, and his service to the khan. The Tatar version emphasizes Chura's resistance against Ivan the Terrible's forces. In the most moving section, Chura is betrayed and killed through treachery, but his sacrifice inspires the Tatar people to continue fighting. The epic is a powerful expression of Tatar national identity and resistance, surviving in multiple versions among Kazan Tatars, Crimean Tatars, Nogais, and Kazakhs.

title: The Legend of the Blacksmith's Daughter
summary: A beloved Tatar legend tells of a poor blacksmith's daughter whose kindness and skill saved her village. When a terrible drought struck the Volga region, all the wells dried up and the crops failed. The blacksmith's daughter, named Chulpan, prayed to Tengri for help. In a dream, she was shown a hidden spring beneath a sacred birch tree. The next day, she led the villagers to the tree, where they dug and found abundant water. The spring never ran dry, even in the driest summers. Grateful villagers believed that Chulpan was blessed by Su Anasi herself for her pure heart. A version of this legend attaches to the Kaban Lake in Kazan, whose name means 'Lake of the Boar' — from the wild boar that supposedly guarded the spring from the blacksmith's daughter's dream.

title: The Kazan Creation Myth
summary: The founding myth of Kazan explains how the city got its name. According to legend, a Bulgar prince was hunting on the banks of the Volga when his party stopped to prepare a meal. They put a copper cauldron (kazan in Tatar) on the fire to boil water. A serpent emerged from the water, warning the prince that a great city would rise on this spot but would face many trials. Frightened, the prince's men fled, leaving the cauldron behind. When they returned, the cauldron had filled with gold coins. The prince interpreted this as a sign from Tengri and founded the city of Kazan on the site. Another version connects the city's foundation to Queen Söyembikä, who threw a golden cauldron into the river while fleeing enemies, marking the spot where the city would be built.

title: The Altai Creation Myth
summary: The Altai creation myth is the foundational cosmology of the Altai Turks. In the beginning, there was only water — a primordial ocean covering everything. The supreme god Kayra Han (also Bai-Ulgen) sent a loon (or in some versions, a golden bird called galbingaa) to dive to the bottom of the ocean to bring up mud. From this mud, Kayra Han created the earth. He then planted the sacred tree Ulukayın (Bay-Terek), which grew with nine branches representing the nine heavens. From the roots of this tree, nine human ancestors emerged, forming the nine races of humanity. Kayra Han created the god Ulgen for goodness and Erlik for the underworld, establishing the dualistic balance between light and darkness. When Erlik tried to rival Kayra Han, he was cast down to the underworld where he rules as the god of death. The myth was recorded by Wilhelm Radloff in the 19th century.

title: The Epic of Maadai-Kara
summary: Maadai-Kara is one of the greatest Altai heroic epics, narrating a thousand-year struggle compressed into two generations. The epic begins with the hero Maadai-Kara and his people suffering under the oppression of the underworld lord Erlik. The central hero, Kögüdei Mergen (son of Maadai-Kara), undertakes a shamanic journey into the underworld to unlock the secret of life and death. He must bring his parents back from the dead, rescue his livestock and people from destruction, and defeat the dark forces of Erlik. The epic unfolds across three cosmic dimensions — the earth (middle world), the underworld (spirit realm), and the heavens (divine realm). The hero is aided by the sacred tree Bay-Terek, magical winged horses (argamak), and the supreme god Yuch-Kurbustan. Female shamanic figures like Abram Moos Kara Taaci (Erlik's daughter) play crucial roles as guides and challengers.

title: The Epic of Ak-Kaan
summary: Ak-Kaan ('White Khan') is an Altai heroic epic telling of a shining white khan who brought justice to the Altai mountains. Born with a divine radiance, Ak-Kaan could see through deception and hear lies hidden in words. His quest was to defeat the dark shamans serving Erlik and restore balance between the upper and lower worlds. With his magical horse (argamak) that could fly between the three worlds, Ak-Kaan traveled to the court of Bai-Ulgen in the sky to receive divine weapons. He returned to defeat the seventy-seven demons of Erlik and establish the 'White Faith' (Ak Jang) — the Burkhanist religion that blends shamanism with Buddhist and Tibetan influences. The epic reflects the transition from pure shamanism to Burkhanism among the Altai people in the 19th and early 20th centuries.

title: The Legend of the Jade Mountain
summary: Deep in the Altai mountains lies a sacred peak called the Jade Mountain (or Mountain of the Green Stone). According to Altai legend, the mountain was once a giant jade serpent that protected the Altai people from invaders. When enemies massed at the foot of the mountains, the serpent raised itself and turned to stone, creating an impassable barrier of green jade. The spirit of the serpent still lives within the mountain, and the jade stones found in Altai rivers are fragments of its scales. Altai shamans use jade in their rituals, believing it connects them to the earth's power. The legend emphasizes the sacredness of the Altai landscape — every mountain, river, and spring has its own 'eezi' (spirit master) that must be respected through offerings of white ribbons tied to trees.

title: The Shaman's Journey to the Spirit World
summary: Altai legend tells of the first shaman, named Jaangara, who was taught the art of kamlanie (shamanic journeying) by Erlik himself. According to the story, a nobleman fell ill and called upon Jaangara to heal him. As Jaangara drummed and entered a trance, his spirit traveled up the sacred tree Bay-Terek to the sky world, where he spoke with Bai-Ulgen, and then descended its roots to the underworld court of Erlik. Erlik offered him the power to heal in exchange for a sacrifice. Jaangara accepted but was forever bound to serve both worlds — bringing healing from the upper gods and knowledge of death from the lower spirits. The Altai people say that real shamans (kamlars) still make this journey today, guided by their spirit-helpers (ongons) and accompanied by the sound of the topshuur (two-stringed lute).

title: Ochi-Bala and Kan-Altyn
summary: Ochi-Bala and Kan-Altyn are two of the most important Altai heroic tales, recorded from the legendary kaichy (epic singer) Aleksei Kalkin in the 20th century. Ochi-Bala tells of a warrior woman of extraordinary skill who defends her people with a bow made of birch and arrows tipped with the teeth of defeated demons. Kan-Altyn ('Blood-Gold') recounts a hero who must retrieve a magical golden sword from the underworld to save his people from an ice demon that freezes the land. The hero travels through the underworld on a winged horse, receives aid from the spirit of his ancestor, and battles Erlik's ice minions. Both epics emphasize the reciprocal relationship between the Altai people and their landscape — the hero's power comes not from individual strength but from harmony with the spirits of mountains, rivers, and ancestors.

title: Nyurgun Bootur the Swift
summary: The most well-known Olonkho epic of the Yakut (Sakha) people, consisting of over 36,000 verses. It tells the story of the hero Nyurgun Bootur, a warrior of the Aiyy (good) tribe who protects the Middle World (human realm) from the evil Abaasy spirits of the Lower World. The epic explores the balance between three cosmic worlds — Upper (gods), Middle (humans), and Lower (devils) — and features elaborate descriptions of the hero's battles, his celestial origins, and his role as protector of the Uraankhai Sakha tribe. Recorded by Platon Oyunsky in the 1920s-1930s, it is considered a defining text of Yakut national identity and was recognized by UNESCO as a Masterpiece of Oral and Intangible Heritage.

title: Er Sogotokh (The Lonely Man)
summary: A foundational Yakut Olonkho epic about Er Sogotokh, the first human hero created by the supreme deity. Born alone in the Middle World, he must contend with the hostile Abaasy spirits from the Lower World who seek to destroy him. After achieving victory over the evil forces, he finds a wife from the Upper World and establishes the first human tribe. The epic explores themes of solitude, divine purpose, and the establishment of human society in a hostile cosmos. It contains some of the oldest layers of Yakut mythology, predating the northward migration of the Sakha people.

title: Kyys Debiliye (The Warrior Maiden)
summary: A rare Yakut Olonkho epic featuring a female warrior as its main protagonist. Kyys Debiliye is a powerful heroine who fights alongside and against male heroes, demonstrating the full range of martial prowess and determination. She defends her people against the evil Abaasy spirits and enemies that threaten the Middle World. The epic is notable for its strong female central character in a traditionally male-dominated genre and reflects the high status women could hold in Yakut mythology. It was published in the series 'Monuments of Folklore of the Peoples of Siberia and the Far East'.

title: The Legend of the Mammoth (Great Underground Bull)
summary: A Yakut legend explaining the origin of mammoth tusks found in the Siberian permafrost. According to the myth, the mammoth is a great underground bull that lives beneath the earth and only emerges when it dies. The creature is so massive that its movements cause the earth to shake and create sinkholes. Yakuts believed that mammoths were giant creatures that had lived underground for millennia, shunning the sun's light. When their bones and tusks were uncovered by erosion, they were seen as evidence of these subterranean titans. The legend reflects the Yakut people's observations of frozen mammoth remains emerging from melting permafrost along riverbanks.

title: Chyskhaan, the Snow Spirit
summary: The Yakut legend of Chyskhaan, the powerful spirit of winter and cold who rules over the frozen landscape of Siberia. Chyskhaan is depicted as a towering figure with icy breath that can freeze anything instantly and a heart made of permafrost. He is both feared and respected by the Sakha people, as he governs the long, brutal winters that define life in Yakutia. Offerings are made to appease him before the onset of winter. In modern Sakha culture, Chyskhaan has been adopted as a regional Father Frost figure (similar to Ded Moroz) while retaining his ancient animistic origins as the personification of the extreme cold that can reach -60°C.

title: The Diving Duck Creation Myth
summary: The Yakut creation myth about a diving duck that brought the first earth from the bottom of the primordial sea. In the beginning, there was only water. The supreme deity sent a diving duck (or loon) down into the depths to retrieve mud from the ocean floor. The bird succeeded in bringing up a small piece of earth, which the deity then caused to expand and grow into the world as it exists today. This classic 'Earth Diver' myth is widespread among Siberian peoples and represents the Yakut variations on this ancient cosmogonic theme. The legend reflects the importance of waterfowl in Yakut culture and the deep connection between the people and their riverine environment.

title: Sodani the Great Shaman
summary: The Evenki epic tale of Sodani, the most powerful shaman who ever lived, who could journey between the Upper, Middle, and Lower Worlds. The story follows Sodani's initiation by spirits, his epic journey to the Lower World to retrieve a lost soul, and his battle against hostile spirits that threaten the Evenki people. He is aided by his animal spirit helpers including the reindeer, bear, and eagle. The tale illustrates the Evenki shamanic worldview where humans are but a small piece of the natural world and must maintain balance with all beings — animals, plants, rivers, mountains, and stars all have spirits that must be respected for harmony to prevail.

title: The First Fire Stolen from the Sky
summary: An Evenki myth explaining how humans first obtained fire. In the beginning, the ancestors of the Evenki lived in eternal cold without fire. A brave shaman or culture hero transformed into a bird and flew up to the sky, where he stole a burning ember from the celestial realm. He brought the fire down to the frozen taiga, giving warmth, light, and the ability to cook food to humanity. The spirits of the sky were angered by this theft and sent storms and lightning to punish the earth. This myth belongs to the widespread 'stolen fire' tradition found across many northern cultures and emphasizes the Evenki value of bravery and sacrifice for the community good.

title: The Moose and the Sun
summary: An Evenki legend explaining the journey of the sun across the sky. The story tells of a celestial moose that carries the sun on its antlers as it runs across the sky each day. At night, the moose rests and the sun sinks below the horizon. However, every evening a great wolf or bear chases the moose, trying to devour the sun. When the predator catches the moose, a solar eclipse occurs. The Evenki shamans would perform rituals and make noise to scare away the predator and free the sun. This myth reflects the central importance of the reindeer and moose to Evenki subsistence culture while also providing a cosmological explanation for solar phenomena.

title: Kuutkh (Raven) Creates the World
summary: The central creation myth of the Chukchi, Koryak, and Itelmen peoples of northeastern Siberia. Kuutkh, the Great Raven, is both creator and trickster who shaped the cosmos from primordial chaos. In the beginning, there was nothing but darkness and water. Kuutkh flew over the endless sea and, through his actions — purposeful and accidental — created land, mountains, rivers, and valleys. He brought light to the world, taught humans essential survival skills including fire-making, hunting techniques, and net-weaving. Kuutkh can shift between raven and human form and his unpredictable nature, blending divine intention with mischievous impulse, mirrors the volatility of the Arctic environment itself.

title: The Raven and the Whale
summary: A Chukchi story from the Kuutkh cycle describing how the Raven creator interacted with the primordial whale. In this tale, Kuutkh encounters a giant whale that threatens the newly created world. Through a combination of trickery and negotiation, the Raven convinces the whale to accept boundaries for its territory, establishing the separation of sea and land. The whale becomes the ruler of the ocean while the Raven takes dominion over the land and sky. This myth establishes the cosmological order of the Chukchi world, explaining why whales remain in the ocean and ravens on land. It reflects the importance of both reindeer herding (inland) and sea mammal hunting (coastal) in Chukchi subsistence.

title: The First Reindeer
summary: A Chukchi legend explaining the origin of the domesticated reindeer, essential to Chukchi survival in the Arctic tundra. According to the myth, Kuutkh created the reindeer from the scattered hairs of his own body or from the remnants of a celestial hunt. The reindeer was given to humanity as a gift from the sky spirits, providing transportation, food, clothing, and shelter in the harsh Arctic environment. The maritime Chukchi, who hunt sea mammals, have a different version where the first reindeer emerged from the sea, blending elements of coastal and inland traditions. The story reinforces the sacred bond between the Chukchi people and their reindeer herds.

title: The Sun, Moon and Firmament
summary: A Chukchi astral myth describing the origins and relationships of celestial bodies. The Sun is considered the most important celestial being, spoken of as an individual spirit with great power. The Moon is seen as a male figure, often described as the 'evil spirit' counterpart of the Sun. Chukchi reindeer herders still seek the influence of specific constellations when making important sacrifices. The myth includes stories of the North Star (a central pillar of the sky), Betelgeuse, and other celestial bodies that guide hunters and herders across the featureless tundra. The firmament itself is envisioned as a solid dome connected to the earth at the horizon, with holes through which spirits and celestial beings pass.

title: Yamal Creation from the Mammoth
summary: A Nenets creation myth from the Yamal Peninsula, whose name means 'End of the Land' in the Nenets language. The legend tells that the world was formed from the body of a giant mammoth that emerged from the primordial sea. The great sky god Num sent the mammoth to create dry land for humans and reindeer. When the mammoth died, its massive body became the Yamal Peninsula, its bones became the Ural Mountains, and its great tusks formed the rivers. Ice spirits (Yul-Chyn) emerged from the mammoth's frozen breath. The Nenets traditionally believed that earthquakes were caused by the mammoth stirring beneath the permafrost. This myth explains the origin of frozen mammoth carcasses that periodically emerge from melting tundra.

title: Num and Nga: The Duel of Creation
summary: The central Nenets dualistic creation myth about the sky god Num and his evil counterpart Nga. According to the legend, Num created the world out of chaos, shaping the land and sky, populating the earth with reindeer and people who lived in happiness. But Nga, the deity of destruction and death, constantly tried to undo Num's work, introducing disease, misfortune, and mortality into the human world. Their eternal struggle explains the coexistence of good and evil in the Nenets universe. Shamans play a crucial role in this cosmic battle, acting as intermediaries who can invoke Num's power to counter Nga's harmful influence through rituals, sacrifices, and journeys to the spirit world.

title: The Seven Reindeer of the Sky
summary: A Nenets celestial myth about seven magical reindeer that traverse the sky, forming the constellation that guides Nenets reindeer herders on their seasonal migrations. According to the legend, the seven reindeer were originally earthly animals belonging to a great shaman who could fly. When the shaman ascended to the sky to negotiate with Num for better conditions for his people, the reindeer followed him and were transformed into stars. Their yearly path across the heavens marks the seasons for the Nenets — when they appear in the east, spring is coming; when they are overhead during the longest nights, winter has reached its peak. The story also emphasizes the sacred status of the number seven in Nenets culture.

title: Abai Geser the Mighty (Buryat Epic)
summary: The Buryat version of the great Central Asian epic of Geser (Geser), focusing on his battles with various demons rather than military campaigns. The epic begins with a unique prologue: the great Tengri Khormusta of the western celestial tribes wars with Atai Ulan, lord of the eastern evil gods. After victory, Khormusta dismembers Atai Ulan and throws his body parts to Earth, where they become monsters and demons that nearly destroy humanity. The middle son of Khormusta, Bukhe Belligte, is sent from heaven as Abai Geser to save the world. The Buryat version contains nine branches or song episodes, each devoted to defeating a different enemy including giant rats, steel ravens, a sun-eating monster, and various multi-headed demons.

title: The Creation of Lake Baikal
summary: A Buryat legend explaining the origin of Lake Baikal, the world's deepest and oldest lake. According to the myth, there was once a great earthquake — the mightiest ever seen — which created a massive crack in the earth's surface. Fire and lava erupted from the crack, threatening to destroy everything. The Buryat people cried out 'Bai Gal! Bai Gal!' meaning 'Stop Fire! Stop Fire!' and the flames ceased. Water then filled the vast depression, forming Lake Baikal. The Buryats consider Baikal a sacred living entity, calling it 'Baikal the Father.' The lake is home to countless spirits and is the dwelling place of powerful deities including the water spirit Usan Khatan and the great dragon-like Luus spirits.

title: The Angara River's Escape
summary: A famous Buryat legend about the Angara River, the only river that flows out of Lake Baikal. According to the tale, Baikal had many sons (the rivers that flow into the lake) and one beautiful daughter named Angara. Baikal, a stern and possessive father, kept Angara imprisoned beneath a great rock to prevent her from leaving. But Angara fell in love with the hero Yenisei (the great river) and escaped. Enraged, Baikal threw a massive rock (Shaman Stone) after her, but she had already reached her beloved Yenisei. The shaman-stone still stands at the Angara's source. Baikal's tears from losing his daughter formed the lake's great depth. This legend is deeply embedded in Buryat cultural identity and is frequently retold in art and literature.

title: Bukha Noyon the Bull God
summary: A Buryat myth about Bukha Noyon, the sacred bull god who descended from the sky to create the world. In the beginning, the sky god sent Bukha Noyon, a massive celestial bull, down to the primordial waters. The bull dredged up mud from the bottom with his horns, which the creator used to form the earth. Bukha Noyon then became the progenitor of the Buryat people, particularly the western Buryat clans. He is associated with fertility, strength, and the mountains of the Baikal region. Sacred sites dedicated to Bukha Noyon still exist in Buryatia, where traditional offerings are made. The bull is considered the totemic ancestor and protector of the Buryat people, and his legend reinforces the strong pastoral traditions of Buryat nomadic culture.

title: The Buryat Earth-Diver Creation
summary: The classic Buryat creation myth — an earth-diver story similar to those found across Central Asia. In the beginning, the sky god Sombov looked down upon the primordial waters and saw the water bird Anghir. He commanded the bird to dive to the depths and retrieve material for making the earth. Anghir brought up black earth and red clay. Sombov made the earth from the black soil and humans from the red clay. These first humans were covered in wool. Sombov left a furless dog to guard his creations while he went to heaven for souls. The devil Shiktur (Erlik) approached the shivering dog, promised it a warm coat, and upon gaining access, spat on the humans — causing their wool to disappear except in certain places. This explains both human imperfection and why dogs still shiver despite their fur.

title: Epic of Gesar of Ling
summary: The great Tibetan epic of King Gesar, the longest living epic in the world with over one million verses and 120 volumes. The story recounts the miraculous birth of Gesar, son of deities sent to earth to vanquish demons, depose the powerful, and aid the weak. He has a despised childhood where he appears as an ugly, snotty child called Joru before winning a horse race at age 12 to become king and marry the beautiful 'Brugmo. Mounted on his celestial steed Kyang Go Karkar, he wages campaigns against demon kings, retrieves his kidnapped wife from the King of Hor, and in his eighties descends to Hell before ascending back to heaven. Rooted in pre-Buddhist Bon traditions and dated to the 12th century, it is sung by bard-shamans across Tibet, Mongolia, and Central Asia.

title: The Cosmic Egg Creation (Bon Tradition)
summary: The pre-Buddhist Tibetan Bon creation myth describing the origin of the universe from a cosmic egg. According to this tradition, in the beginning there was only emptiness. From the void emerged five pure lights, which condensed into a luminous egg. From this cosmic egg hatched the primordial being, who then created the world by separating sky from earth. The egg's white shell became the heavens, its yolk became the earth, and the liquid within became the oceans. Mountains, rivers, and all living beings arose from different parts of the egg's contents. This creation account is preserved in the Bon Kangyur and Tengyur sacred texts and represents Tibet's indigenous cosmology predating the introduction of Buddhism in the 7th century.

title: The Yeti (Migoi) Legends
summary: Tibetan legends of the Yeti (Migoi or Migyur, meaning 'wild man'), a powerful apelike creature said to inhabit the high Himalayas. In Tibetan folklore, the Yeti is not merely a physical creature but a spiritual guardian of the mountains, often associated with mountain gods like Nyenchen Thanglha. Some traditions describe it as a flesh-and-blood being, others as a supernatural creature that can become invisible at will. Stories tell of encounters with the Yeti by pilgrims, herders, and travelers in remote mountain passes, where it must be appeased with offerings. Buddhist lamas have traditionally performed rituals to pacify the Yeti's potentially dangerous nature. The legend has been adopted into Western popular culture but remains a living part of Tibetan Himalayan folklore.

title: Songtsen Gampo and the Founding of Tibet
summary: The semi-legendary account of King Songtsen Gampo (7th century CE), the founder of the Tibetan Empire and the first Dharma King. According to Tibetan tradition, he was an emanation of the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, sent to bring civilization and Buddhism to the Tibetan people. He built the Jokhang Temple in Lhasa, subdued the demoness that lay beneath the Tibetan landscape by constructing 108 temples on her body's pressure points, and invented the Tibetan script by sending his minister Thonmi Sambhota to India. His marriage to Princess Wencheng of China and Princess Bhrikuti of Nepal is said to have brought Buddhism to Tibet. The story of Songtsen Gampo blends historical events with elaborate mythological elements that define Tibetan Buddhist identity.

title: The First Prayer Flag
summary: A Tibetan legend explaining the origin of the iconic Tibetan prayer flags (Lungta or Wind Horse flags). According to the tradition, when the Buddha's teachings were first spreading in Tibet, harmful spirits and negative energies threatened the people. The great master Padmasambhava (Guru Rinpoche) used prayer flags as a technique to transform negative energy into positive blessings. The mantras and prayers printed on the flags, when touched by the wind, spread blessings across the land like a horse racing through the sky. The five colors of the flags — blue (sky), white (air), red (fire), green (water), yellow (earth) — represent the five elements of balance. The central Wind Horse symbol carries the wish-fulfilling jewel of enlightenment on its back.

title: The Tertön (Treasure Revealer) Traditions
summary: Tibetan legends of the tertöns — treasure revealers who discovered hidden spiritual texts and sacred objects (terma) concealed by Padmasambhava and his consort Yeshe Tsogyal in the 8th century. According to tradition, Padmasambhava foresaw the persecution of Buddhism that would occur in Tibet centuries later and hid thousands of teachings, ritual objects, and sacred images in lakes, caves, rocks, and the minds of future disciples. These hidden treasures would be discovered at the prophesied time by specially destined tertöns, who would have been the original disciples of Padmasambhava in a previous life. Major tertöns include Nyangrel Nyima Ozer, Guru Chöwang, and the famous Jamyang Khyentse Wangpo. The terma tradition remains active in Tibetan Buddhism today.

title: Mountain God Nyenchen Thanglha
summary: The Tibetan legend of Nyenchen Thanglha, one of the most powerful mountain gods in Tibetan mythology. He is the spirit of the Nyenchen Thanglha mountain range in central Tibet, revered as a protector deity who commands armies of elemental spirits. In pre-Buddhist Bon tradition, he was a wrathful, dangerous spirit who demanded blood sacrifices. When Padmasambhava brought Buddhism to Tibet in the 8th century, he famously subdued Nyenchen Thanglha and bound him by oath to protect the Buddhist teachings. Today he appears in Buddhist iconography as a white-skinned warrior riding a white horse, holding a banner and jewel. He is considered the chief of all 'nyen' mountain spirits and is called upon for protection, success in endeavors, and favorable weather.

title: The Origin of Throat Singing (Khöömei)
summary: The Tuvan legend explaining the origin of khöömei throat singing, the unique overtone singing technique of the Tuvan people recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage. According to the myth, long ago a young Tuvan hunter became lost in the Sayan Mountains during a blizzard. Desperate and alone, he began to mimic the sounds of his surroundings — the howling wind, the rushing rivers, and the calls of animals. He discovered that by manipulating his throat and breath, he could produce multiple sounds simultaneously. The mountain spirits were so pleased that they blessed him with this gift to share with his people. Another version says that throat singing was first taught to humans by the spirits of the mountains themselves, who sang the songs of the earth, wind, and water through the human voice. Historically, the practice is deeply connected to Tuvan animism and pastoral life.

title: Khan Töölö the Hero
summary: The Tuvan epic of Khan Töölö, one of the most important figures in Tuvan oral tradition. The epic tells of a heroic warrior-prince who defends the Tuvan people against invading forces and monstrous beings from the Sayan and Altai mountains. Khan Töölö possesses supernatural strength granted by the sky spirits and rides a magical horse that can speak and fly. The epic is traditionally performed with throat singing accompaniment, with different vocal styles representing different characters — deep kargyraa for the hero, high sygyt for maidens, and rhythmic khoomei for battle scenes. The story reinforces Tuvan values of bravery, hospitality, respect for nature, and the sacred bond between a hero and his horse. The epic can take many hours to perform in its complete form.

title: Merger the Hero (Nanai Epic)
summary: The central Nanai epic hero Merger, whose adventures form the core of Nanai oral tradition. The epic follows Merger as he journeys along the Amur River, battling evil spirits, demons, and rival clans to protect the Nanai people. He is aided by his shamanic powers, his supernatural strength, and the spirits of the Amur River and Siberian taiga. The story incorporates elements of shamanic initiation, shape-shifting (into tigers, birds, and fish), and journeys to the underworld. Merger's quests often involve retrieving stolen souls, restoring balance to the natural world, and defeating the 'water demons' that lurk in the deep pools of the Amur. The epic is traditionally performed by Nanai shamans during important ceremonies and can last for several nights of continuous recitation.

title: The Amur River Spirit
summary: A Nanai legend about the powerful spirit of the Amur River, known as the 'Dragon River' or Amur Father. According to tradition, the Amur is a living entity with its own will and consciousness. The river spirit can be generous or wrathful, providing abundant fish to those who respect it and drowning those who offend it. Shamans would perform rituals on the riverbanks to communicate with the Amur spirit, making offerings of millet, tobacco, and rings. The legend tells of the spirit appearing as a giant fish, a serpent-dragon, or an old man with a long white beard emerging from the river's waters. The Amur is considered the source of all life for the Nanai people, and it is taboo to speak disrespectfully of the river or to pollute its waters without making proper offerings.

title: Raven the Creator (Nivkh Tradition)
summary: The Nivkh creation myth centered on the Raven, a central figure shared with neighboring Chukchi and Koryak traditions. In Nivkh mythology, the Raven (known as Kurka or similar names) created the world by diving into the primordial sea and bringing up earth on his beak. He shaped the land of Sakhalin Island and the Amur River estuary, and then created human beings from clay mixed with his own feathers. The Raven is both creator and trickster who taught the Nivkh people how to fish, build canoes, and conduct the sacred bear ceremony. The myths also explain how the Raven brought the sun and moon into the sky by stealing them from a powerful spirit. Nivkh folklore was first systematically recorded by Bronisław Piłsudski and later by Vladimir Sangi.

title: The Bear Ceremony (Chakhlynd)
summary: The central Nivkh myth explaining the origin of the bear ceremony (chakhlynd), the most important ritual in Nivkh culture. The legend tells of a woman who married a bear in human form. She returned to her village with her bear-children, and the bear was killed by her relatives. Before dying, the bear promised that if people would honor bears in a special ceremony, the bear spirits would provide protection, good hunting, and prosperity. The ceremony involves raising a bear cub in a special enclosure for several years, treating it as an honored guest, before sacrificing it in a carefully ritualized manner. The bear's spirit is believed to return to the mountain god and report on how it was treated. The ceremony, which can last several days, includes elaborate singing, dancing, dog sacrifices, and feasting. It reinforces the sacred relationship between humans, animals, and the spirit world.

title: The Sea Spirit Tolk
summary: A Nivkh legend about Tol (or Tolk), the powerful sea spirit who governs the waters around Sakhalin Island and the Sea of Okhotsk. Tolk is a massive creature that controls the movement of fish, the intensity of storms, and the success of sea mammal hunts. Before embarking on sea voyages, Nivkh hunters and fishermen would make offerings to Tolk — throwing tobacco, food, and sacred objects into the sea to ensure his favor. Tolk could appear as a giant sea serpent, a whale of impossible size, or a terrifying humanoid figure rising from the waves. If angered, Tolk would capsize boats, hide fish from nets, and send storms to punish the community. The spirit is associated with the cycle of the seasons, as Tolk's moods shift with the ice and currents of the northern Pacific. Shamanic rituals were performed to divine Tolk's will.

title: The Shaman's Canoe Journey to the Underworld
summary: A Nanai shamanic epic describing a powerful shaman's journey in his spirit-canoe to the underworld (buni) to retrieve a dying person's soul. According to Nanai tradition, the underworld is a mirror of the world above but reversed — the rivers flow backward and the seasons are opposite. The shaman must navigate treacherous waters in his magical canoe, defeat hostile spirits, pass through a narrow strait guarded by monstrous creatures, and bargain with the lord of the underworld for the soul's release. The journey takes many days and the shaman must leave his physical body in a trance state. If the shaman fails, he may die himself or become trapped in the underworld. The tale illustrates the Nanai concept of soul dualism — a person has multiple souls, one of which can wander during dreams or be stolen by spirits.

title: The Ghost of the Lhamo (Tibetan Wrathful Deities)
summary: Tibetan legends of the Lhamo — powerful female wrathful deities who serve as protectors of the Buddhist teachings. The most famous is Palden Lhamo (Glorious Goddess), the principal female protector deity of Tibetan Buddhism. According to legend, she was a wild, blood-drinking demoness who was converted to Buddhism by the great saint Machik Lapdrön or by Padmasambhava himself. She rides a mule through a sea of blood, her saddle made from the flayed skin of her own son whom she killed to prevent him from betraying the dharma. The Lhamo traditions bridge Tibetan Buddhism and the earlier Bon animistic beliefs, representing the fierce, protective aspects of feminine spiritual power. Offerings and rituals to Palden Lhamo are performed to this day, especially during the New Year ceremonies in Lhasa.
